entry.title: Re: SFM CCF - Dynamically distribute emissions to product...
entry.content:
Hello Paul_H,

Thank you for detailing your setup so clearly! The behavior and the errors you are experiencing are actually expected and align with the standard design of the SFM calculation engine.

To answer your main question directly: Machine/Resource times from Internal Service Confirmations are not enough on their own for the system to distribute the Total Energy Consumption.

Here is the technical logic behind this: While the Internal Service Confirmations provide the duration of usage (how many hours a machine ran), the calculation engine still needs to understand the energy intensity of each specific resource. For example, the system cannot assume that a heavy industrial machine and a low-voltage assembly line consume the same amount of energy per hour.

To solve this, the engine requires the Planned Energy Consumption Rates. The system uses these planned rates as a baseline, multiplies them by the real work times, and derives an accurate distribution factor. This weighting factor is what allows the total energy bill (or macro meter reading) to be dynamically and proportionally distributed among all resources month over month.

How to resolve this: You have two architectural paths according to the official standard design:

  1. Use Planned Rates (Recommended for your setup): You need to configure the Manage Planned Energy Consumption Rates app for the 4 resources showing errors. This is usually a one-time setup. Once entered, the system will use them alongside your actual machine hours to dynamically distribute the actual emissions to the product level without fixed allocation ratios.

  2. Energy Flow Model Using Only Meters: The only way to completely skip Planned Rates is if you have individual sub-meters physically installed and configured for each of your production resources in the Energy Flow Model, feeding actual data into the GHG Data Form for every single machine.

For further learning on how these calculation methods and distribution factors are structured, please refer to the official SAP Help documentation:

  • Overview of all calculation scenarios: Energy Distribution Scenarios

  • How distribution factors are calculated specifically for your scenario: Energy Flow Model Using Only Planned Energy Consumption Rates

I hope this helps clarify the calculation logic and helps you clear those errors!

Best regards,

Kauê Da Hora 
SAP Technical Support PC ERP (SCT/SFM)

entry.content:
Hello,

If you actually run this script "HANA_Configuration_Parameters_2.00.040+.txt" (Note ##1969700) on tenant level there will be a "recommended_value" column suggestion if HANA determines that the current "default_statement_concurrency_limit" is misconfigured with consideration for the number of "cpu_threads" on the physical server.
(M_HOST_INFORMATION view)


Kind Regards
Simon

entry.title: Sales Order creation via EDI using API - New Customer Part creates useless TATX line.
entry.content:
We are implementing S4/HANA on private cloud.

We are loading customer standard order from EDI using the API.

Several customer only order using the buyer part number.

The issue is when the customer orders a new part where we haven't set up a CMIR the line loads as a TATX line with the new buyer part in the Customer Material field.

OK, but this is next to useless. Sure we can figure out what they are ordering and set up a CMIR but then what?

The line has no quantity so the CSR doesn't know HOW much they ordered.

The CSR has no way to reprocess the order to the item becomes a normal item.

We have no idea what the next step is for this kind of error.

entry.title: Re: Getting Error: Parameterized views can't be used with...
entry.content:
Hello, the JSON should look like this: 

 

// Default (local development) — use SQLite
"requires": {
"db": {
"kind": "sqlite",
"credentials": { "database": ":memory:" }
}
},

// Hybrid profile — use HANA with explicit dialect
"[hybrid]": {
"requires": {
"db": { "kind": "hana" }
},
"sql": {
"dialect": "hana" // ← THIS IS THE CRITICAL MISSING LINE
}
},

// Production profile — use HANA with explicit dialect
"[production]": {
"requires": {
"db": { "kind": "hana" }
},
"sql": {
"dialect": "hana" // ← Required here too
}
}
}

entry.title: Data Model EHS Classic
entry.content:
Introduction

EHS Classic allows lot of options to manage data. We have data elements like

  • identifier
  • Specification header
  • Specific topics as Reference / Inheritance
  • Component type / exception values
  • Phrase based characteristics
  • Regulatory LIst based characteristics
  • Other types of characteristics

and much more

Depending on you starting point: there are some rules you should use to avoid a crash of your data model.

Assumption is now: you use 3E / SAP Standard model

Hints of topics to avoid

  • For phrase based characteristic: do not add customer specific phrases to the phrase set; only if you are sure that the characteristic is not used by a 3E rule set: you should do a mix of 3E and customer specific phrases
  • Do not add e.g. own compontent types / exception values or own Regulatory lists as long as you now: what is 3E Standard and what is the reason to add something new
  • Do never manipulate SAP EHS classes/characteristics
  • If needed: always "copy" the SAP class to a customer specific one
  • Keep in mind the VERY important topic of "Unit of Measurement"; if you are not taking care here: you will get a mess
  • You should only carefully adapt the 3E original WWI templates to your need and you should have a good documentation what was changed and you need potentially a strategy: hwo to make updates whicih happens in 3E WWI templates and copy them to your customer specific one
  • You should use a "most primitive set up" of your system. There are so many options to enhance the system; at some point in time you might not have the overview what was changed

What you can do with low risk

  • prepare own new value assignment tyes plus if needed classes/characteritics
  • prepare own property tree
  • Define whatever kind of strategy for using the "Substance Authority group"
  • If you would like to use your customer specific topics in a customer specific WWI template: yo can do so

For Superusers

If you have understood all the options provided by SAP you can do a clever set up to support the maintenance in many places. But you need to check: what option is the best. As one example: you can prepare a "dyanmic phrase set" strategy; but there is much more you can use if needed.

And if you are the "super super" user: you can completely change look and feel in CG02/CG02BD.

entry.title: Re: Week 4: AI Developer Challenge SAP-RPT-1 Model
entry.content:
Used RPT-1 model to predict sales group for laptop and chair first which is by default in the sales file. Predicted pretty good but when i added notebook (as a stationary product in the dataset , its prediction got deviated, generating wrong sales group). 

I think the reason being there was not relevant dataset available which Notebook product can take reference of. so i have added Marker product as a stationary sales group and again predict the sales group for Notebook. 

This time RPT-1 model found out the relevant reference and predict correctly.

Excited to see how this model evolves further and help in daily analytics to business on the structures reporting scenarios. Thanks @KevinR  for this whole challenge weeks.

entry.title: Lessons from the field: What Early Adopters of SAP IBP’s Harmonized Planning Area learned
entry.content:
In September 2025, SAP launched an Early Adopter Care (EAC) program for customers implementing the Harmonized Planning Area (HPA) in SAP IBP. Over 7+ months, 15+ companies across industries joined weekly knowledge-exchange sessions with SAP Product Management, Development, and expert consultants — covering integration architecture, data model deep-dives, and go-live preparation. This blog distills those lessons for the benefit of a wider IBP user community. 

 The Participants — A diverse cross-industry group 

The program attracted 15+ companies from Automotive, Food & Beverage, Life Sciences, Chemicals, High Tech, Consumer Goods, and Agriculture — spanning EMEA and Americas, from mid-size players to global multinationals. Planning scope ranged from a single module to five deployed simultaneously. Integration strategies spanned the full spectrum: RTI-only, CI-only and hybrid. 

Why the Participants joined the initiative: 

Participants joined the HPA EAC for different reasons, but several themes emerged repeatedly: 

  • Greenfield HPA: Starting fresh on IBP and choosing HPA from day one rather than implementing a legacy planning area. 
  • New business unit rollout: Companies already on SAPIBP1 piloting HPA in a second business unit before broader migration. 
  • APO replacement: Early access to understand gaps and plan the transition away from SAP APO. 
  • Exclusive capabilities: Features like harmonized scenarios, vendor-managed inventory exist only in HPA — no alternative for companies with these requirements. 
  • Product influence: A direct channel to SAP Product Management to raise requirements and shape the roadmap while HPA was still maturing.  

Integration decisions — RTI, CI, or both? 

One of the most impactful decisions every HPA customer faces is their integration strategy. The EAC revealed that there is no one-size-fits-all answer — and that most customers end up with a hybrid approach, at least initially. 

The Three Options: 

Technology 

Best for 

Limitations 

RTI (Real-Time Integration) 

Standard master data, OBP transactions, vendor data, delta integration 

No public cloud support; limited to SAP systems; plant-independent requirements not supported 

CI (Cloud Integration) 

Multi-ERP landscapes, non-SAP systems, flexible transformations, public cloud 

Application job template integration was not available during the EAC program 

CIDS (Cloud Integration Data Services) 

Existing implementations, custom enrichment, backward compatibility 

Legacy technology; no longer bundled for new customers; limited tracing 

 

Case Study 1: The CI-Only Approach: A customer made the bold decision to use Cloud Integration exclusively — no RTI at all. Their rationale: 

  • RTI does not work with public cloud deployments or non-SAP ERP systems 
  • They integrate with 4+ different ERP systems globally — RTI only connects to SAP 
  • Internal expertise for CI was more available than for RTI 
  • CI enables trial operations before full S/4HANA migration 

Their architecture: Custom CDS views in S/4HANA handles all data transformation. CI flows are kept “dumb” — pure data transportation with no business logic. This separation of concerns makes debugging and maintenance straightforward. 

Case Study 2: The Hybrid Approach: This customer uses RTI for base master data integration (the standard objects that RTI handles well) and enriches with CIDS for additional attributes that require custom logic on the S/4HANA side. This is a pragmatic approach when you need RTI’s delta capabilities but also have custom fields that RTI bodies don’t cover. 

Key learning — CI vs. CIDS: One customer who migrated from CIDS to SAP CI was really happy with migration. They cited better flexibility, superior tracing, no data agent performance issues, and easier team collaboration since CI skills are not limited to IBP specialists. 

RTI recommendation from SAP: SAP’s integration strategy recommendation for new customers is to go RTI first. This gives customers delta integration capabilities which is not available with time-series methods. RTI also future-proofs the integration — if customer starts with time series and later adopts OBP, the RTI integration is already available. 

Value realization: 

Despite being early adopters on a maturing platform, participants reported concrete value across several dimensions: 

First Customer Live on HPA: In March 2026, a participant became one of the first customers to go live on the Harmonized Planning Area — with SOP, Inventory Optimization, and Time Series Optimizer all active simultaneously. Some of the key values realized by these early adopters are: 

  • Configuration simplification: Customers discovered that with introduction of IBP_CONVERSION function, HPA eliminates the need of helper key figures and additional planning levels for UOM conversion. The system converts automatically without any helpers KFs. Pre-configured conversions cover most use cases. 
  • Unified planning model: Participants who activated both time-series and order-based planning in a single HPA confirmed the core value proposition: consistent results across both planning methods, unified key figures, and no reconciliation required between separate planning areas. One participant preparing for a June 2026 go-live is running TS + OBP + Inventory Optimization + S&OP + Demand & Response Planning — all in one planning area. 

Challenges and concerns raised 

The EAC program is valuable precisely because participants surface real challenges — not just success stories. Here are the most significant concerns raised, presented honestly for the benefit of future adopters. 

Some of the key concerns raised by participants: 

  • Subnetwork filtering in TS optimizer: One consumer goods company cannot limit the time-series optimizer to a specific factory — it runs across the entire global supply chain. Without subnetwork filtering (planned for 2611), planners cannot run optimization at their scope.  
  • Cross-location vendor mapping: An automotive company operating in 60–70 countries discovered that RTI’s inbound mapping allows only one vendor code per source location. With multiple subsidiaries ordering from the same distribution center using different vendor codes, they cannot build their supply network.  
  • Scheduling agreements as source of supply: Critical for automotive planning but not available in HPA until 2608. 

 How SAP supported EAC participants:  

One of the defining characteristics of the EAC format is that customer challenges are shared directly with the product managers and developers who can act on them. SAP team responded to customer concerns by updating product roadmap. Some features committed to Roadmap based on customer inputs are: 

Customer needs

SAP response 

Planned release 

Subnetwork filtering in TS optimizer 

Confirmed on roadmap 

IBP 2611 

Scheduling agreements for Response & Supply 

Confirmed on roadmap 

IBP 2608 

Cross-location vendor mapping in RTI 

Roadmap to extend mapping table with role and location info 

2605–2608 

Look-up logic for calendars/attributes 

Delivered 

IBP 2602 

Receiving calendar hierarchy inheritance 

Part of hierarchical lookup feature 

IBP 2602 

 Migration Support announced:  SAP7F Migration EAC: A dedicated EAC program for brownfield customers migrating from SAP7F to HPA, is planned to start with the 2605 release.  If you are interested, please register with following link : Influence Opportunity Homepage - Customer Influence

Best Practice content delivered: In direct response to EAC participant requests, SAP delivered executable best practice content in IBP 2511 — including planning views, analytic stories, and planning profiles that customers could use immediately rather than building from scratch. 

Real-Time issue resolution: Pattern observed throughout the EAC: When customers raised specific technical issues (e.g., attribute naming bugs, OKF behavior questions, lot-for-lot procedure gaps), SAP development teams often investigated and responded within 1–2 sessions — far faster than the standard support ticket process. 

Looking forward — Recommendations for future adopters 

Drawing on seven months of EAC discussions, here are the distilled recommendations for customers considering HPA adoption: 

  1. Start small with partial-scope copy : You do not need to adopt every HPA module at once. When copying I_SAPIBP2, select only what you need — Demand Planning and IO is a common starting point. Expand your footprint as you gain confidence. 
  2. Choose your integration strategy early: The RTI vs. CI decision has cascading effects on your architecture, team skills, and timeline.  
  3. Leverage coexistence for phased transition: HPA and SAP7F planning areas can run side-by-side on the same tenant using separate logical systems. This means you can validate HPA in a contained scope before decommissioning legacy areas — reducing risk significantly. 
  4. Plan for the upgrade lifecycle from day one: When you copy I_SAPIBP2, keep the original sample planning area untouched as a reference for future comparison. When new releases deliver additional content, use the planning area comparison tool to identify deltas and merge selectively. 
  5. Engage with SAP early: Customers in the EAC consistently received faster issue resolution, influenced the product roadmap directly, and benefited from peer learning that would have taken months to discover independently. If an EAC program exists for a capability you’re adopting, join it. 
  6. Best practice content Is your accelerator: Since IBP 2511, SAP has delivered executable planning views, analytic stories, and job profiles for HPA. Use them as your starting point rather than building from scratch. EAC participants who used the pre-release content reported significant time savings in their setup. 

Summary: 

 HPA is where SAP IBP is heading, and the early adopters in this program showed the way. They validated that the model works, the integration options are viable, and the value proposition is real — leaving a clear path for those who follow.

entry.title: How to Prepare a Successful OpenText VIM Landscape Upgrade
entry.content:
• 1. What Is OpenText VIM Versioning?
  • 2. Compatibility Check with SAP S/4HANA release version
  • 3. OCR / Capture Component Compatibility Check
  • 4.Validation Client and the Shift to Fiori App Capture Validation Workplace
  • 5. OpenText Archive Center and VIM Upgrade
  • 6. VIM Foundation and Invoice Solution Upgrade (excluding Beyond Invoice component)
  • 7. Concluded VIM Upgrade Preparation Framework

1. What Is OpenText VIM Versioning?

OpenText VIM versioning follows release numbers such as 7.6, 20.4, 23.4, 24.4, and 25.4. These versions are not only product labels. They indicate which SAP platform versions, add-ons, Fiori components, capture integrations, and support package levels are expected to work together.


A simple way to understand VIM versioning is:

VIM generationTypical SAP platform relationshipGeneral meaning
VIM 7.6SAP ERP / ECC-oriented landscapesOlder generation, still relevant in legacy ERP environments
VIM 20.4Earlier SAP S/4HANA releasesOften used in S/4HANA 2020–2022 landscapes
VIM 23.4S/4HANA 2023 generationTransitional release with more modern Fiori and Foundation capabilities
VIM 25.4S/4HANA 2025 generationTarget release for latest S/4HANA 2025 compatibility and newer functions


This is the most important message for beginners: VIM versioning must be read together with the SAP S/4HANA version. You cannot safely decide the VIM target version without first confirming the SAP target version.

2. Compatibility Check with SAP S/4HANA release version 

The first step in any VIM upgrade preparation is to confirm the target SAP S/4HANA release.

This is important because VIM is installed as an SAP system add-on, and each VIM version supports specific SAP S/4HANA releases. Therefore, the VIM target version should be decided based on the SAP target release, not independently.

For example, if the SAP system is being upgraded to SAP S/4HANA 2025, the corresponding VIM version must be 25.4, according to SAP Note 3654390: https://me.sap.com/notes/3654390.

If the SAP system remains on an older S/4HANA release, the latest VIM version may not be compatible.

3. OCR / Capture Component Compatibility Check

The OCR and capture area can be confusing because OpenText product names have changed across generations.

Before upgrading, do not rely only on the name used by the customer.

TopicComponent / NameWhat it meansUpgrade attention point
BCCBCCOlder Business Center Capture generation used in legacy ECC / VIM landscapes.VIM 25.4 does not supported any BCC/ICC components anymore
IES (Information Extraction Services)IC4S / C4SOn-premise Capture for SAP Solutions based on IES. IC4S and C4S refer to the same product line across naming changes.Check Windows Server, IIS, .NET, learning data, capture profiles, and Validation Client compatibility.
Core Capture / CC4SCloud-based IES capture optionCheck cloud readiness
Supporting Plug-InCapture Imaging Plug-InSupporting imaging component for capture/rendering. 

Always check the installed product, version, deployment model, and compatibility with the target VIM Foundation version.

4. Validation Client and the Shift to Fiori App Capture Validation Workplace

The Validation Client is used to review and correct OCR extraction results before documents continue in the VIM process.

For upgrade preparation, the key rule is simple: keep the Validation Client on the same release level as VIM Foundation and the OCR/Capture component. 

As an alternative, customers can also use the Fiori Capture Validation Workplace for browser-based validation. This reduces dependency on local Windows client installation and aligns validation with the modern Fiori user experience.

In short: align the Validation Client version with VIM and Capture, or consider Fiori validation as the modern alternative.

5. OpenText Archive Center and VIM Upgrade

OpenText Archive Center, or OTAC, is used to store invoice images and attachments.

VIM does not store the physical document itself; VIM manages the invoice process, while the archive repository stores the document content. The connection is usually handled through SAP ArchiveLink:

ComponentRole
VIMManages invoice metadata, workflow, business rules, approval, and posting.
OTAC / Archive RepositoryStores and retrieves invoice images and attachments.
SAP ArchiveLinkLinks the SAP/VIM document record to the stored document image.

For upgrade preparation, there is generally no direct version compatibility dependency between VIM and OTAC.

6. VIM Foundation and Invoice Solution Upgrade (excluding Beyond Invoice component) 

For VIM upgrade preparation, the key point is to identify which SAP add-ons are installed today and which add-ons are required for the target VIM version.

ComponentExample add-on name in VIM 25.4Role
VIM FoundationOTVIMBASBase framework for inbound processing, capture integration, workflow, and Fiori foundation.
VIM Invoice SolutionOTVIMINVInvoice-specific processing, including PO/non-PO invoices, business rules, approvals, exceptions, and posting.
VIM Fiori UIOTVIMFUIProvides Fiori apps and UI framework for VIM users.
Optional scenario add-onsFor example, Ariba or TM-related VIM add-onsSupports additional integration scenarios such as Ariba CIG or Transportation Management.

SAINT (SAP Transaction): VIM add-ons are usually installed or upgraded by the SAP Basis team using SAINT, the SAP Add-On Installation Tool. 

7. Concluded VIM Upgrade Preparation Framework

Use below checklist as a VIM upgrade preparation framework:

Use this order as the upgrade readiness checklist:

  1. SAP S/4HANA target release
    Which SAP S/4HANA version will be used?
  2. VIM target version
    Which VIM Foundation and VIM Invoice Solution version is required?
  3. Capture / OCR model
    Will capture stay on-premise, or move to cloud capture? What OCR solution version will be adopted?
  4. Validation approach
    Will users validate invoices in Windows Validation Client, Fiori Validation Workplace, or both?
  5. Fiori deployment model
    Will VIM Fiori apps be deployed on-premise or via SAP BTP?
  6. Archive repository
    Which archive repository and ArchiveLink setup will be used?
  7. Language and country scope
    Which languages and countries are in scope for AP users, approvers, and validators?
  8. Integration scope
    Which integrations must remain active, such as email, scan, IDoc, Ariba, TM, DRC, or custom APIs?

 

Good luck to have a smooth and successful OpenText VIM upgrade.

Read more of my article on VIM introduction: VIM Foundation & Invoice Solution Configuration Ov... - SAP Community

entry.title: Salary from start date of Bonus Plan
entry.content:
Hi All, 

I have a scenario where I need to consider the salary of eligible employees starting from the plan’s start date. For example, I have a bonus plan that runs for three years (2024–2026). In this case, I need to use the salary effective as of January 1, 2024, along with the target percentage from that same date, rather than using values as of December 31, 2026. Can this be achieved using point‑in‑time functionality? If not, what alternative approach would you suggest? 

@DavidSomelofske  @xavierlegarrec @johnking : Looking forward for your suggestion. 

Thanks 

Ritu

entry.title: SAP career transition guidance
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,
I am exploring a career transition into SAP consulting and would greatly appreciate insights from experienced professionals and consultants in this forum.
A brief background about myself:
30 years old | CMA Inter | 7 years of experience in taxation within manufacturing MNCs
Work spans corporate tax compliance, tax reporting, and related areas across multiple jurisdictions
Active SAP end user throughout my career, working regularly with finance and tax-related modules
I have the following questions specifically around SAP PAPM (Profitability and Performance Management):
Is transitioning into SAP PAPM consulting advisable at my current stage, given my age and finance/tax background?
What does a realistic career trajectory in SAP PAPM consulting look like for someone from a tax/finance domain?
What roadmap would you recommend — certification sequence, practical exposure, and positioning for consulting roles?
How does the SAP PAPM job market look over the next 10–15 years, given S/4HANA adoption and the shift to cloud?
Are there realistic opportunities for onsite roles or international postings within SAP PAPM consulting?
How does SAP PAPM complement other SAP finance modules, and is it a strong standalone specialisation?
I understand everyone here is busy, and even a brief response or a pointer in the right direction would be truly helpful.
Thank you in advance for your time and guidance.

entry.title: The condition class in S4 HANA is not created BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2 but it is in R3
entry.content:
Good morning everyone.
Currently, my client is in the process of migrating to S/4HANA. We have a program Z that creates sales orders (VA01) through the BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2. The program fills in all the required fields of the BAPI (it enters four condition classes per item), but after creating the order, it does so without the ZCGM condition, which is manually configured as mandatory for a specific order class, ZC06. If I create the order manually using VA01, SAP prompts me to enter the ZCGM condition class since it is mandatory, and it creates the other classes without problems.

The curious thing here is that this same BAPI is used in R/3, and the same fields are filled in, but the order is created using the ZCGM condition class.

I have seen that at the configuration level, everything is OK in both R/3 and S/4HANA. I performed standard debugging on the BAPI and verified that the condition is never lost, but for some reason, it's not created in S4 HANA when other condition classes are added. If only the ZCGM class is used, it creates it without problems, but the client needs to add other condition classes as well.

S4 HANA order created through BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2  (The ZCGM condition is not present.)

Order created in R3 with the same data and using BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2( if created with condition ZCGM )

 

Data that I enter in conditions for the BAPI in both S4 HANA and R3 environments.

We have also tried setting a value in LOGICAL_SWITCH-PRICING but it doesn't work for us in S4 HANA either.

entry.title: Re: Supplier quotation creation using BAPI or FM in S4 HA...
entry.content:
Hi Sudheer,

Thanks for your valuable suggestions. I achieved the Supplier Quotation creation using following steps.
     Main API is API_QTN_PROCESS_SRV.
    1.Create supplier Quoation by calling API : API_QTN_PROCESS_SRV/CreateFromRFQ?QuotationSubmissionDate=datetime{ <lv_date>}Supplier=<lv_lifnr>&RequestForQuotation=<lv_rfq_no>.  

     Set up Http post method for above url.
      Once get the http status code 200 or 201, add the Supplier quotation line item with Quantity and Price using below API.
      API_QTN_PROCESS_SRV/A_SupplierQuotationItem(SupplierQuotation=<lv_quot_no>,SupplierQuotationItem=<lv_item_no>.  This is PATCH method
     After add the all line items, the Quotation will be submitted using following API.
     API_QTN_PROCESS_SRV/Submit?SupplierQuotation<lv_quot_no>.  - This is also Https post method.

Thanks,
Venkat.

entry.title: Re: SF Learning: Online Session timeout
entry.content:
Hello.
Please note that an enhancement request has been submitted regarding this: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#idea/263029

Also, within the LMS system configuration under AUTHENTICATION you can change the value of the session time out. We changed ours to 14400 (seconds) to match the 4 hour timeout that our RISE content is set at on the content server.

# The value of HTTPSessionTimeout is passed to HTTPSession.setMaxInactiveInterval() each time a session is created.
# As of Servlets 2.2-2.5, that implementation accepts values specified in seconds. For example: 1800 is 30 minutes.
# This overrides the value specified in web.xml for the session timeout (in the session-config/session-timeout element).
# The minimum value for httpSessionTimeout is 1800 seconds, and the upper limit is 28800 seconds.
httpSessionTimeout=14400

Also, within LMS system configuration under LMS_ADMIN please ensure you have your keep alive interval set to a lower rate to allow more pings to be sent to platform to keep the session "alive".

# The Online Content Structure page has a LMSsession keep alive feature that is based on this amount of time (in seconds).
# Default: 300 (5 min)
# 0 - Session timeout-600 (i.e. 10 minutes based on the 30 min session time out); Minimum : 120 (i.e. 2 minutes)
contentStructurePageKeepAliveInterval=250
# This is an opt in setting to extend the User session to 4 hours while taking the AICC content.
# It will reset back to normal however when user navigates back to the home page, i.e. away from the content launching page.
extendAiccOnlineSession=true

I hope this helps.

Tom

entry.title: Re: Posting date being updated to today’s date when uploa...
entry.content:
We had a same error when we upgraded our system to S4HANA to 2025 version. This issue is due to OBD1 setting. Workflow is looking into OBD1 setting in 2025 version.

Set OBD1 to blank for the respective company codes. Hope it helps

entry.title: Re: Posting date being updated to today’s date when uploa...

entry.title: Building an AI Agent for SAP Maintenance using MCP, CAP and Claude Desktop
entry.content:
From Transactions to Conversations: Connecting Claude Desktop to SAP S/4HANA via MCP

A few weekends ago I sat down with a question I've been carrying for months: how fast is generative AI actually going to change the way we interact with SAP? Not in the marketing sense. In the day-to-day, "open IW38, set the filters, export to Excel" sense.

I didn't want to write another opinion piece. I wanted to build something and see for myself.

This post is the result: a working proof of concept, two short videos of it running, and an honest reflection on what I think it means for everyone in the SAP world, users, developers, consultants, support teams.

 Why I Built This

The trigger was the Model Context Protocol (MCP), the open standard Anthropic released to let AI assistants talk to external systems through a clean, structured interface. The moment I understood how it worked, one thought wouldn't leave me alone: if Claude Desktop can call any tool, why is it still asking people to navigate transactions to get SAP data?

So I picked the SAP module I know best, Plant Maintenance, and built a bridge.

 What the Project Does

The proof of concept lets a user ask Claude Desktop questions like these, in natural language, and get answers pulled live from SAP S/4HANA:

  • "I have a meeting in 5 minutes about EDDY Pumps. Give me a quick summary of all open orders."
  • "Is there any order related to an oil leak? What's planned to fix it?"
  • "Equipment 10001949, does it have more than one open order? Show me all of them."
  • "Which orders have priority 1 in plant 1010?"

No SAP GUI. No Fiori. No transaction codes. No filter screens. The user just asks.

See It Running

I recorded two short videos showing the assistant pulling real data from S/4HANA:

Video 1 - Summary before a meeting:


Video 2 - Investigating a leak:

 The Architecture (Briefly)

 

For those who want the technical picture:

  • SAP CAP on Node.js exposes an MCP-compliant HTTP endpoint on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry
    A small local script (mcp-stdio.js) acts as a stdio bridge between Claude Desktop and the BTP server
    The server queries SAP S/4HANA via OData (using SAP Business Accelerator Hub for the sandbox)
    Claude Desktop sees it as a standard MCP server with tools it can call

  •  

get_maintenance_orders
get_maintenance_order_detail
get_maintenance_order_operations

At runtime, Claude Desktop sees a small set of domain-specific tools exposed via MCP:

The full code, deploy steps, and configuration example are in the repo. Lessons learned (CAP routing quirks, Express middleware gotchas, why you need the stdio proxy at all) are documented there too.

  • Step 2 - configure Claude Desktop

    Open %APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json and add:

 

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "sap-maintenance": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": [
        "C:\\path\\to\\mcp-maintenance-cap\\mcp-stdio.js"
      ]
    }
  }
}

 Now the Honest Part What This Could Mean for the SAP World

This is where I want to slow down. Because the demo is fun, but the implications are bigger.

For End Users
The maintenance manager who today depends on someone, or on remembering filter combinations she learned five years ago, to pull a report? She just asks. The supervisor who wanted to know which equipment has the most open notifications but never had time to learn the t-code? He just asks.

This is, unambiguously, a win for the user. The system finally bends to the human, instead of the other way around.

 For Developers and Consultants

Here's where it gets interesting, and where I find myself thinking out loud.

If a well-designed AI agent can answer most operational questions through natural language, what happens to the parts of our work that revolve around teaching users to navigate the system, building the 47th custom report, or writing the "how to filter IW38" page of a training manual?

I don't think those things disappear overnight. Enterprise software doesn't move at consumer-AI speed. But I do think the shape of the work changes, and it changes faster than most of us are planning for.

 For Configuration, Training, Projects, and Support

A few honest questions I'm sitting with:

  • If an AI agent can explain why an order is in a given status by reading the master data on the fly, how much classic L1/L2 support is still needed?
  • If users no longer need to learn screens, how much of a typical training plan survives?
  • If a consultant's value moves from "I know where the field is" to "I know how to design the agent and the data behind it," what does the next generation of SAP consultant look like?
  • When does this happen, 2 years, 5 years, 10 years? I genuinely don't know. Anyone who tells you they do is selling something.

 What's Next

The same pattern works for any SAP module. The maintenance use case was where I started because it's mine, but you can imagine equivalents for MM (stock and POs), SD (orders and deliveries), QM (inspections), or FI/CO (cost centers and postings). The architecture doesn't care which OData service is on the other end.

I'd love to see this turn into a community conversation. What modules would you try first? What does the "agent-first SAP" workflow look like in your area? Where do you see the real risks?

Try It, Break It, Tell Me About It
The full project code, deploy steps, lessons learned, and videos are open on GitHub:

https://github.com/bernardcaldas/mcp-maintenance-cap 

Suggestions, pull requests, and issues are very welcome on the repo. Critiques, corrections, and "you missed this" are equally welcome.

We're early. None of us has the full picture. But I'd rather build small things and learn in public than wait for someone else to define what AI + SAP looks like.

Thanks for reading.

entry.title: Re: BDC scroll issue AB01
entry.content:
Hello @xhulioa14 ,

Your issue happens because this screen is a table control, and in BDC SAP only reads the visible rows, so values like 93 and 95 are not reached automatically. The OK code =P+ alone does not work unless you trigger the scroll, reload the screen (dynpro), and then fill the next set of visible fields again. The correct approach is to either use scroll + reprocessing of the screen or control the scroll using BDC_CURSOR + =P+, then populate the fields after the scroll. In short: BDC does not auto-scroll table controls—you must explicitly scroll and then re-enter data for the newly visible rows.

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: SAP career transition guidance
entry.content:
Hello @ramanathan_seshan6969cf ,

This is just an advice from my side. Do your own due diligence. Yes, your transition is absolutely feasible at 30, and your strong tax and finance background is a major advantage—but starting directly with SAP PAPM is not ideal because it is a niche and advanced module; the practical path is to first build a solid foundation in SAP FI (Financial Accounting) and CO (Controlling), where most entry and mid-level opportunities exist, and then move into PAPM as a specialization focused on profitability modeling, allocations, and advanced financial analytics; the job market for SAP Finance will remain strong for the next decade, while PAPM roles are fewer but high-value, especially when combined with FI/CO expertise, and international opportunities typically come once you establish yourself in core finance modules; therefore, position yourself as a finance/tax professional transitioning into SAP Finance consulting and later specializing in PAPM, rather than approaching PAPM as a standalone entry point.

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: Migrating to SAP HANA Cloud: What Actually Gets Better (Part 1 of 2)
entry.content:
@chetan_099 

When I reference S/4HANA and HANA Cloud integration in that section, I'm referring specifically to on-premise S/4HANA scenarios where HANA Cloud is being introduced as a separate analytical or data layer alongside the existing on-premise HANA database — not as a replacement for it. In those cases, integration work (SDI, SDA, or replication flows) is genuinely required to move or federate data between the two environments.

Your point on S/4HANA Cloud Public and Private Edition is correct — both run on HANA Cloud natively as their underlying database, so there's no separate integration needed at the DB layer. The "integration" that still exists in those scenarios is more about connecting HANA Cloud to external analytical tools, SAP Datasphere, or non-SAP data sources — not between S/4 and HANA Cloud itself.

entry.title: Re: SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store (Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
@chetan_099 

Really good question and it gets at a genuine architectural choice worth thinking through carefully.

No, you don't always have to embed. Embedding + vector retrieval is the right pattern when your use case requires semantic similarity, finding conceptually related content across a large corpus where the exact field values aren't known upfront. Classic RAG scenarios: policy documents, knowledge bases, unstructured notes, transport descriptions.

For S/4HANA semi-structured data: material masters, purchase orders, cost center hierarchies — you're often better served by structured retrieval first. Query the data directly via OData or RFC, filter precisely on known fields, and pass the retrieved records as context to the model. No embedding required, no vector store overhead, and you get exact matches rather than approximate similarity.

Where RPT-1 fits into this: SAP's RPT-1 model is optimized for structured tabular SAP data — it reasons over rows and columns natively without needing the data pre-embedded. For S/4 transactional data queries where you know the structure and just need intelligent interpretation of the results, RPT-1 with direct data retrieval is genuinely more efficient than an embedding pipeline.

The pattern I'd recommend for S/4 semi-structured data: use structured retrieval (OData/RFC) to fetch the relevant records, pass them directly to RPT-1 or your chosen model as context, and reserve the embedding pipeline for genuinely unstructured content — documentation, ABAP code comments, email threads, support tickets — where semantic search adds real value over exact field matching.

Embedding everything by default is a common over-engineering trap in SAP RAG implementations. Match the retrieval pattern to the data structure.

entry.title: Header: Inconsistent with the runtime object
entry.content:
Hi All
I created a custom table and later changed a data element. After this change, errors started appearing, and I was not able to add or delete fields in the table.
then I deleted the table and tried to recreate it, but the table could not be activated properly and errors continued.
Error is: No active name tab exists for ZTEST_T1 Termination due to inconsistencies Table ZTEST_T1(Statements could not be generated) Error number in DD_DECIDE (9).
To fix this, I created a new table first and then copied it using the old table name. After running the required function module(DD_TABL_ACTM), the table was activated successfully and the SE14 database adjustment was completed.
Now, the table works at runtime. I am able to activate the table and enter data without any issues. However, in SE14 → Database Object Check, one field is still shown as inconsistent (in red). This field was already deleted earlier. Also, for this specific table name only, I am not able to add or delete fields. Copying the table works, but modifying the structure does not.
If I create the same table with a different name, everything works fine. Please advise how to permanently resolve the database object inconsistency and confirm whether it is safe to transport this object to the QA system.
Runtime object check: Consistent Please find the attached screenshot. SE14 database object check: Shows field inconsistency Please find the attached screenshot.

 


Note: SAP system is ECC and database system is DB6. (SAP_BASIS :740)

Thanks,
venkat.

entry.title: Smarter Compliance Starts Here: BTP Compliance Manager Meets SAP Document Reporting & Compliance
entry.content:
Smarter Compliance Starts Here: BTP Compliance Manager Meets SAP Document Reporting & Compliance 

Crimson Partners' BTP Compliance Manager ingests any data format, leverages API connections and/or SAP Event Mesh, and delivers external documents seamlessly into SAP Document Reporting & Compliance — so your team can leverage SAP Document Reporting & Compliance as your sole E-invoicing and statutory reporting solution for all your ERP systems, SAP and non-SAP. 

Why this matters for your compliance team 

  • Faster submission cycles — Real-time and batch ingestion replace manual file handling, cutting preparation time significantly. 
  • Reduced manual intervention — Automated mapping and validation means fewer errors reaching SAP Document Reporting & Compliance and fewer corrections after submission. 
  • Multi-source flexibility — Consolidate data from subsidiaries, legacy systems, and cloud platforms without a custom integration project for each. 
  • Audit-ready by design — Every transformation step is logged, giving you a complete lineage from source record to SAP Document Reporting & Compliance submission. 

SAP-native and secure — Built on BTP, the solution inherits SAP's enterprise security, identity management, and governance controls.

Consume any format, from anywhere 

Most compliance platforms are built around a single, rigid data model. If your source data doesn't conform, you spend weeks on transformation projects before a single report is filed. The BTP Compliance Manager takes a fundamentally different approach. 

Whether your finance team exports Excel workbooks, your ERP streams XML, or a subsidiary drops flat CSV files onto a shared drive — the platform accepts them all, natively. 

No pre-processing. No brittle middleware to maintain. The ingestion layer handles format detection and validation automatically, flagging anomalies before they reach your compliance pipeline. 

Event-driven at its core: SAP Event Mesh integration 

Static file uploads are only part of the story. For organisations running on SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP services, or any event-producing system, the BTP Compliance Manager connects natively to SAP Event Mesh — the enterprise messaging backbone of the SAP Business Technology Platform. 

This event-driven architecture unlocks near-real-time compliance visibility. Compliance teams no longer need to wait for overnight jobs or month-end extracts. The data arrives as it's created in the sources systems, verified, and routed automatically. 

Intelligent transformation into SAP Document Reporting & Compliance 

Ingesting raw data is step one. What happens next is where the real value is delivered. The BTP Compliance Manager applies a configurable transformation engine that maps incoming data — whatever its original structure — to the exact schema required by SAP Document and Reporting Compliance. 

END-TO-END DATA JOURNEY 

* Available as on-demand integration. Scoped per client requirements. 

The transformation layer handles field mapping, data enrichment, currency conversions, date normalisation, and validation against SAP Document Reporting & Compliance business rules — all before a single record reaches the reporting engine. Mapping templates can be configured per entity, legal jurisdiction, or data source, giving compliance teams granular control without requiring developer involvement for every change. 

Error handling is built in throughout. Records that fail validation are quarantined, logged with a clear reason code, and surfaced in a review queue in the Compliance Manager — meaning your SAP Document Reporting & Compliance submissions always contain clean, verified data. 

The compliance pipeline 

Every document goes through the same five stages. Each is a hard gate. A document that does not pass goes no further, and the submitter gets a clear explanation of what failed.

By the time a document reaches SAP Document Reporting & Compliance it has passed security screening, format conversion, schema validation, and country-specific business rule checks. What arrives is a verified, compliant external document. 

Custom transformation layer 

No two enterprise landscapes look the same. Source systems across different ERP platforms, procurement tools, and subsidiaries each produce invoice data in their own format. Expecting every system to conform before onboarding is where most compliance projects stall. 

 

The Crimson Compliance Manager includes a custom transformation layer built to adapt to each client’s existing setup. When a document arrives in a source format that differs from the e-invoicing standard for that country, the platform converts it automatically before validation runs. Transformation is configured per country and per client. Support for new formats can be added on demand, with no changes to the core application.

Technical architecture, solution for AR and AP 

External documents from ERP systems can be connected to the Compliance Manager via API/Event Mesh (for high volumes) or CSV/Excel uploads (for low volumes) to pass the data for billing documents/E-reports to SAP Document Reporting & Compliance. From there, the client reviews and submits to the relevant tax authority through the standard SAP Document Reporting & Compliance workflow. 

The platform handles SAP communication securely through BTP’s connectivity layer. Authentication is managed automatically. The process from submission to SAP Document Reporting & Compliance delivery runs without manual intervention once a country is configured. 

For countries such as Saudi Arabia and Oman where B2C E-invoicing is required, the POS systems can be connected via APIs to the Compliance Manager to pass the simplified customer invoice data to SAP Document Reporting & Compliance (for simplified and robust integration) for E-invoicing automatically and in real-time/batches. 

For countries with E-reporting requirements such as France, finance data required for E-reporting can be extracted from source systems using the same methods and converted to the data format for SAP Document Reporting & Compliance to generate and submit the E-reports. 

For vendor invoices and AP processes in non-SAP systems, the E-invoices received in the SAP Document Reporting & Compliance will be passed back to the ERP systems via Compliance Manager, which will convert the data to the format required by the ERP/Vendor Invoice Management systems and carry out 3-way matching if required. SAP Document Reporting & Compliance is not where the vendor invoices will be processed or rejected, but rather a conduit to receive vendor E-invoices. The vendor invoice processing will continue to be carried out in the ERP/Vendor Invoice Management systems or in BTP. 

Deployment options, SaaS or On-Premise 

The Crimson Compliance Manager is available in two deployment models depending on your organisation’s infrastructure preferences and data requirements. 

What this means in practice 

Built for enterprise, ready for what comes next 

The Crimson Compliance Manager is built for organisations managing compliance across multiple countries, entities, and source systems. A single pipeline handles the full journey from document intake to SAP Document Reporting & Compliance delivery, with country rules and transformation settings managed centrally. When a new market goes live or a regulation changes, the platform adapts without disrupting what is already working. 

As E-invoicing networks continue to expand across more markets, the platform is designed to grow with them. The same compliance pipeline that handles direct integrations today is built to accommodate broader network connectivity as those requirements evolve. 

Do check out the blog post here :- https://crimson-partners.com/smarter-compliance-starts-here-btp-compliance-manager-meets-sap-document-reporting-compliance/

entry.title: Re: Cannot create Custom Fields for Material Document Hea...
entry.content:
Hi LBONAR22,

This questioned sounded familiar, in doing some research, seems I answered it last year 🙂 

Add Custom Field in Material Document Header Level

In that answer, I suggested to the OP to file an influence request.

We also suggested to consider another object like Journal Entry, but in your case, maybe the delivery or delivery item?

In your case, could you make use of the Material Item and create your fields there.

How many Material Items do you typically have on the Material Doc? if you have typically 1, there is not much difference from having the field defined on the header.

You can use the BADI "MMIM_ITEM_PROVIDE_DATA" to update the custom fields. It also seems to have already the fields you need to reference (reference and header fields, etc.) to pull your data.

You want to modify the MMIM_GR_LABEL_E form correct? That form has the Item node, and the Custom Field can already populate there.

I hope this helps

Thank you

Jerry

entry.title: The Challenges You Face and How SAP Business Data Cloud Helps
entry.content:
Data stewards are accountable for data quality, compliance, and trust, but operate across fragmented systems, manual processes, and growing regulatory pressure—often without clear ownership or visibility.  For data stewards, SAP Business Data Cloud is not “more governance tooling.” It is a way to operationalize governance, scale trust, and turn stewardship into a measurable business enabler.

This is where SAP Business Data Cloud changes the conversation. It gives data stewards a governed, business‑ready foundation where business context, semantics, data, and usage all come together. Governance isn’t separate from analytics—it’s embedded in how data is shared and consumed.

Let’s talk a bit about problems and solutions:\

Fragmented data and inconsistent definitions

Your challenge:
Key business data is spread across SAP and non‑SAP systems, often defined differently by different teams. This makes it hard to enforce standards or build confidence in reporting and analytics.

How SAP Business Data Cloud helps:

  • Brings business data together with a consistent semantic layer
  • Helps align technical data with shared business definitions
  • Makes governed, trusted data easier to discover and use across analytics and AI scenarios

What this means for you:
Less time reconciling definitions. More confidence that people are working from the same understanding of the data.

Manual governance and limited visibility

Your challenge:
Governance work often lives in documents, spreadsheets, or disconnected tools, making it hard to track ownership, issues, and progress.

How SAP Business Data Cloud helps:

  • Connects governance concepts directly to live data assets and processes
  • Improves visibility into where data comes from, how it’s used, and who is responsible
  • Supports more consistent, repeatable governance practices instead of one‑off fixes

What this means for you:
You can focus on managing and improving data, not chasing information or enforcing rules manually.

Compliance pressure without operational support

Your challenge:
You’re expected to support regulatory and internal compliance, but controls are often indirect or hard to demonstrate during audits.

How SAP Business Data Cloud helps:

  • Enables clearer lineage, transparency, and traceability across data flows
  • Helps connect governance policies to actual data usage
  • Makes it easier to show how data is managed, protected, and governed in practice

What this means for you:
Stronger audit readiness and reduced risk—without slowing down the business.

Building trust with business and IT stakeholders

Your challenge:
Business users want speed. IT teams want stability. You need both to trust the data and the governance behind it.

How SAP Business Data Cloud helps:

  • Provides a shared foundation where business and technical teams work from the same governed data
  • Supports analytics and AI use cases without bypassing governance
  • Helps you communicate the value of governance in terms business leaders understand

What this means for you:
You gain credibility as a trusted partner, not a blocker—strengthening your influence in the buying and decision‑making process.

Again, it’s not about “more governance.” It’s about giving data stewards the structure, transparency, and leverage they need to protect and elevate the value of enterprise data.

Want to learn more about SAP Business Data Cloud as it applies to your role?  Check out this guide: One Foundation. Every Role. Smarter Insights.

entry.title: Re: SAP Document AI – Quality Certificate processed succe...
entry.content:
Hello!

 

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition does support automatic processing of supplier quality certificates with SAP Document AI. When everything is aligned correctly, the system can:

-                Automatically recognize the quality certificate

-                Match it to the existing Certificate Receipt

-                Attach the document

-                Automatically change the status from “Expected” to “Received”

 

In the current flow, the certificate PDF was uploaded directly in the SAP Document AI portal using a generic (custom) document type and an OCR‑only schema.

This means:

-                Document AI successfully performed OCR and processing

-                SAP S/4HANA QM was not involved in the upload process

-                The system therefore had no trigger to link the document to the Certificate Receipt

As a result, the Certificate Receipt correctly stays in “Expected” status and the document is not attached automatically.

 

For automatic status update and attachment to work, SAP requires the document to be uploaded from within SAP S/4HANA, not directly in the Document AI tenant.

The intended process is below (details in this blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/smarter-quality-management-with-ai-assisted-processing-sap-s-4hana-cloud/ba-p/14184266😞

  1. The certificate receipt is created automatically at Goods Receipt (status = Expected)
  2. The quality certificate is uploaded using the SAP app “Upload Quality Certificate Documents” (app ID: F8858)
  1. SAP then sends the document to Document AI for data extraction
  2. SAP uses the extracted data (PO, item, material, supplier, etc.) to find a match
  3. If a match is found the document is attached and the certificate receipt is automatically set to “Received”

 

If the match cannot be determined clearly, SAP switches to a manual review process.

If automatic matching does not happen (for example, if data is missing or unclear), SAP expects manually to:

-                Review the document

-                Attach it manually if needed

-                Change the certificate status to Received in Manage Certificate Receipts

 

About the “Upload Quality Certificate Documents” it becomes available automatically when:

-                Scope item 7TC (Integration with SAP Document AI) is active

-                The business catalog QM – Incoming Quality Certificates is assigned

-                The user role is refreshed

entry.title: Introduction to Next-Gen SAP Ariba
entry.content:
Introduction


Next-gen SAP Ariba marks a foundational transformation of SAP's procurement platform. It is the first modern, AI-native source-to-pay suite designed for the era of intelligent business operations. This comprehensive modernization, launched with the February 2026 (2602) release, transforms procurement from reactive to proactive, manual to autonomous, and siloed to orchestrated across the entire business ecosystem.
Next-gen SAP Ariba is built on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) using a modern, cloud-native architecture with API-first integration patterns.

Business Benefits:
Procurement Excellence: Streamlined end-to-end processes from strategic sourcing through payment with real-time supplier engagement and advanced integration capabilities
Enhanced Compliance: Guided buying enforces company policies with proactive warnings, automated validation, and comprehensive audit trails
Supplier Collaboration: SAP Business Network facilitates global buyer-supplier connections, supporting ethical supply chain development and transaction efficiency

entry.title: Exploring SQL Console Features in SAP HANA Cloud: Lessons from My Internship!
entry.content:
Hi everyone!

My name is Joshua and I have been a Product Management Intern for the past four months. I had the opportunity to explore a wide range of features and tools used in SAP HANA Cloud Central. During this time, I became particularly interested in SQL tooling and how modern platforms are evolving to make database development more accessible and efficient.

In this post, I want to highlight a few SQL-focused features that stood out to me and that I think are worth exploring further!

The first feature I wanted to highlight is the Gen AI capabilities within the SQL Console in SAP HANA Cloud. As the industry continues to embrace AI-powered tools, it is interesting to see how SAP is applying these capabilities to SQL for development and query creation.

Within the SQL Console, there are three new AI-assisted options: “Run Prompt”, “Explain SQL Statement using AI”, and “Optimize SQL Statement using AI”. Run Prompt allows users to convert natural language into SQL statements, which is especially helpful for new SQL users who may not yet be familiar with syntax. Explain SQL Statement using AI uses AI to translate SQL queries into natural language, making it easier to understand what a query is doing, particularly for those less experienced with SQL. Finally, Optimize SQL Statement using AI helps improve existing queries by suggesting more efficient and readable versions.

Another feature that stood out to me is code completion within the SQL Console. As you type, the console suggests SQL snippets and schema objects in real time. I’ve found this especially helpful when I’m unsure of the exact spelling of schema objects, as it allows me to quickly autofill them without breaking my workflow. It has also made my SQL writing more efficient by reducing the need to manually type out repetitive or complex statements. Overall, it’s a simple addition that makes working with the SQL Console more fluid and less disruptive.

The SQL Console has also recently introduced the ability to restore closed tabs. This is a feature I found particularly useful, as there have been many times when I’ve accidentally closed the wrong tab and lost my SQL queries. In those moments, having to recreate the work can be frustrating and time-consuming. With this new functionality, it’s now possible to quickly restore recently closed tabs, which makes the overall experience much more forgiving and user-friendly. The option can be accessed through the three-dot overflow menu in the top-right corner of the toolbar, and it allows you to restore up to the last 10 tabs within your current session. Overall, this is a small but impactful addition that helps prevent unnecessary rework and makes it easier to recover from simple mistakes. It’s definitely a feature I recommend trying out!

Another feature that I found particularly interesting is the ability to run SQL statements in the background within the SQL Console. This is a capability that previously existed in the Database Explorer, and it has now been brought into the SQL Console experience as well. It’s a feature that can be easy to overlook but becomes especially valuable when working with long-running queries.

With this functionality, users can execute long-running SQL statements independently of their browser session. This means that even if you navigate away, disconnect, or close the tab, the query will continue running uninterrupted. You can then return later to review and download the results once execution is complete.

In addition, the SQL Console introduces a dedicated tab in the results area for background activities. This provides a centralized view where users can monitor the status of ongoing executions, check progress, and access completed results all within the same interface.

Overall, this feature improves the handling of resource-intensive queries by removing the need to remain actively connected while they run. It creates a more flexible workflow and makes it much easier to manage long-running database operations.

The last SQL Console feature I want to highlight is the syntax help button in the toolbar. This is one I found really useful when working through unfamiliar queries, as it brings helpful reference information directly into the console.

The first dropdown, “SQL Statements,” shows the correct structure for the SQL statement you are currently working on. I found this especially helpful when I wasn’t completely sure about the required syntax or formatting. Being able to quickly check the structure and even navigate to additional documentation from within the dropdown made it much easier to learn as I went.

The second dropdown, “Tables and Views,” displays details for the objects referenced in your current SQL statement. I really liked using this to quickly verify which tables or views I was working with, especially when dealing with unfamiliar schemas. It also made it easier to check object names or spelling without leaving the console. The ability to search and insert objects directly, or navigate to them in the Database Objects app, helped streamline my workflow quite a bit.

The third dropdown, “Procedures and Functions,” offers similar functionality for procedures and functions used in your SQL statement. I found it helpful to quickly view parameters and jump directly to the relevant object or documentation for more details.

Overall, exploring these features has given me a much better understanding of how SQL tooling is evolving to become more intuitive and user-friendly. From AI-assisted query generation to quality-of-life improvements within the SQL Console, each of these additions plays a role in making database development more accessible and efficient. 

Throughout my internship, I’ve really enjoyed getting hands-on experience with these tools and seeing how even small enhancements can make a big difference in day-to-day workflows. I’d definitely recommend trying out these features for yourself, as they can meaningfully improve both productivity and the overall development experience once you start using them regularly! 

I’d also love to hear from others, what’s your favorite SQL Console feature, and are there any capabilities you’d like to see added in the future?

entry.title: S4 Post Upgrade - Profile Generator (SU25) Options
entry.content:
Hi All ,

While performing the security upgrade (SU25) as part of the S/4HANA system migration from S/4HANA 2022 SP03 to S/4HANA 2025 SP01, we observed that several new options were introduced in SU25. The impact of these changes is not clearly explained in the SAP documentation available to us. As a result, many of our roles are being affected—new authorization objects are being introduced, some existing ones are being removed automatically, and custom SU24 updates appear to be overwritten.

Before proceeding further, we would appreciate guidance on the impact of each selection under SU25 steps 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, etc. If any detailed documentation or guidance is available, please share it.

As a precaution, we are also considering taking a User Master export post-upgrade, but before executing SU25, along with a backup of the SU24 customizing tables (USOBT_C and USOBX_C). We would like confirmation on whether these backups can be used to restore the system to its pre-SU25 state if required.

entry.title: First Half 2026 Release: What's New in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central?
entry.content:
As organizations continue to navigate evolving workforce expectations and increasingly complex regulatory environments, HR teams need a strong and reliable core that keeps people processes moving while maintaining data accuracy and compliance. With the First Half 2026 release, we are introducing new innovations in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central that help reduce friction in daily HR operations, improve data quality, and support scalable global HR practices.

Alongside foundational core HR enhancements, this release introduces AI agents in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central. These agents are designed to help monitor workflows and data, surface potential issues, and support faster resolution. Let’s take a closer look at the innovations coming with this release.

Faster, More Informed Approvals with Enhanced Workflow Notifications

Approvals are a critical moment in core HR processes, yet approvers often lack the full context needed to act quickly and confidently. This release enhances workflow notifications by bringing pending, to‑be‑approved employee data directly into the notification itself. Instead of navigating into the system to review details, approvers receive the information they need upfront.

Using field mapping, administrators can define variables for these notifications to include pending (to-be-approved) data, providing approvers with the key context they need directly in email. This added transparency reduces back‑and‑forth, accelerates decision‑making, and helps keep HR processes moving smoothly, particularly in fast‑paced or high‑volume environments.

Keeping Workflows Moving with the Pending Workflow Agent

Even with improved notifications, workflows can still stall when requests go unnoticed or approvers are unavailable. This release introduces the Pending Workflow Agent, which is designed to surface workflow status and help keep tasks on track by proactively identifying and addressing bottlenecks.

The Pending Workflow Agent monitors how long requests have been pending and evaluates approver availability to determine when action is needed. It can nudge approvers or reroute requests to delegates or higher‑level managers to avoid delays. This capability is designed to help reduce time‑to‑resolution by up to 40 percent, while minimizing administrative effort and lowering compliance risk.

                                                                                   Pending Workflow Agent 

Improving Data Accuracy with the Employee Data Integration Agent

Accurate and consistent employee data is essential, particularly when supporting payroll and other downstream processes. A new Employee Data Integration Agent enables organizations to detect data inconsistencies across core HR and payroll systems before they create downstream impact.

The agent identifies discrepancies, proposes fixes, and applies approved changes to help keep employee records accurate and aligned across systems. By proactively addressing data issues, organizations can reduce manual rework, avoid payroll disruptions, and maintain a more reliable system of record.

Check out this demo to see the agent in action: Demo: Data Integration Agent

             Data Integration Agent

Simplifying Global Compliance with Country‑ and Region‑Specific Dependents Management

Managing dependent data across countries requires balancing global consistency with local legal requirements. This release introduces enhancements to dependents management to simplify updates for employees and admins while supporting compliance.

Organizations can define country‑ or region‑specific dependent types, objects, and required data fields to meet local legal requirements. Out‑of‑the‑box country settings enable faster implementation, while configuration can be easily modified through a single, unified user interface. By eliminating the need for complicated business rules, HR teams can maintain compliant dependent data more efficiently, while employees benefit from a clearer and more consistent self‑service experience. Furthermore, optimized data management ensures accurate information flows to downstream processes including payroll, benefits, and time management. 

                 Dependents management enhancements

Strengthening the Intelligent HR Core

With these delivered innovations in the 1H 2026 release, we continue to strengthen SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central as the intelligent HR core. Enhancements to approvals, the introduction of AI agents to help monitor workflows and data, and simplified localized compliance all enable more efficient operations today and establish a strong foundation for connected core HR, payroll, and talent processes across SAP SuccessFactors HCM.

Get Started with the 1H 2026 Release

  • Register for the First Half 2026 Customer Release Q&A session for Employee Central to hear directly from SAP experts
  • Explore the What's New Viewer for a complete overview of all SAP SuccessFactors First Half 2026 innovations
  • Visit the Product Release & Road Map Information page to access additional resources, including release assets and product road maps

entry.title: Re: Partial Payment using JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest API
entry.content:
Hello!

 

The error message "Document or line item does not exist for invoice reference" is particularly relevant in the context of partial payments or when dealing with credit memos and subsequent invoices.

Understand the Context: The Invoice Reference field (BSEG-REBZG) is used for line items that are related to another line item. Ensure that you are referencing the correct document number.

Verify that the Invoice Reference you are trying to enter corresponds to an existing document. If the field is empty or does not contain a valid document number, the system will trigger the error.

Ensure that the line item you are referencing is indeed related to the invoice you are processing. This includes checking for credit memos that refer to a specific invoice item or partial payments.

 

In the JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest API, the InvoiceReferenceDocument field should contain only the accounting document number. Company code and fiscal year must be provided in their dedicated fields. If these values are concatenated into the InvoiceReferenceDocument field, SAP cannot resolve the reference and returns the error “Document or line item does not exist for invoice reference.”

  1. Ensure InvoiceReferenceDocument contains only the invoice number
  2. Ensure fiscal year and company code are passed in their own fields
  3. Ensure InvoiceReferenceDocumentItem refers to the customer open item line

 

For example, correct vs. incorrect usage:

Correct structure (API‑compliant):

InvoiceReferenceDocument = "0090000169"   (invoice number only)

InvoiceReferenceDocumentFiscalYear = "2026"

CompanyCode = "BR01"

InvoiceReferenceDocumentItem = "002"

 

Incorrect structure (common cause of the error)

InvoiceReferenceDocument = "190000123410002024"

InvoiceReferenceDocument = "1900001234_1000_2024"

 

Even when all reference fields exist, SAP performs strict line‑item‑level validation for partial payments:

  1. The referenced line item must be the customer receivable open item (not a revenue or tax line).
  2. The line item must still be open (not cleared or reversed).
  3. The line item number must be in internal SAP format (including leading zeros).
  4. The reference must be applied on the customer line of the payment journal entry, not on a G/L line.
  5. The fiscal year must be the accounting fiscal year, not the document or posting date year.

If any of these checks fail, SAP returns the same error message.

entry.title: CAP Developers - Call to Action to mitigate and Apply Solution provided in SAP Note 3747787
entry.content:
On April 29, 2026, four malicious open-source package versions were distributed into the NPM ecosystem. These malicious versions appear to exfiltrate information, such as credentials, and attempt to propagate into downstream software packages as well as adjacent software repositories when installed on a system.

If you are uncertain whether your systems have been affected, it is crucial to act promptly. Begin by following the mitigation steps outlined to maintain your environment’s security. Promptly taking these actions will help protect your systems and data from potential risks.

Other Terms

  • MBT
  • NPM
  • CAP
  • SAP Cloud Application Programming Model
  • MTA Build Tool

List of compromised NPM package versions:

  • · @cap-js/sqlite - v2.2.2
  • · @cap-js/postgres - v2.2.2
  • · @cap-js/db-service - v2.10.1
  • · mbt@1.2.48

Solution

If you have identified that you may be affected, perform the following measures via provided SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003747787

SAP Support Ticket Component : BC-XS-CDX-NJS

Potential references:

  • https://www.aikido.dev/blog/mini-shai-hulud-has-appeared
  • https://www.stepsecurity.io/blog/a-mini-shai-hulud-has-appeared
  • https://www.mend.io/blog/shai-hulud-sap-cap-supply-chain-attack-claude-code/
  • https://onapsis.com/blog/sap-cap-mini-shai-hulud-supply-chain-attack/
  • https://snyk.io/de/blog/bun-based-stealer-hits-sap-cap-js-mbt-npm-packages/

entry.title: Total number of records for complex unmanaged query in RAP
entry.content:
In most of the examples from SAP (https://help.sap.com/docs/ABAP_PLATFORM_NEW/fc4c71aa50014fd1b43721701471913d/254f68c7d5374e94a88241b7ce3bd414.html), the handling of total record count when requested is quite straightforward since there is only a single table involved.

But there maybe some complex reporting scenarios where it is not practical to calculate the total since it will affect the performance. How do you usually handle the total calculation? Do you give a rough estimate of the total number of records or do you really calculate the total whenever it is requested?

The issue with providing a rough estimate is that the value that you pass will usually be reflected at the table header level.
