entry.title: Re: Sales Organization address fields in in Email Templates
entry.content:
Hello!

The fields related to sales organization address can be found in the following path:

  • Sales Area > Sales Organization > Address

In this node, the following fields can be accessed:

  • _SalesArea._SalesOrganization._Address.BusinessPartnerName1
  • _SalesArea._SalesOrganization._Address.BusinessPartnerName2
  • ...

You can also use the search for finding fields you'd like to add in your email template, there are over 10.000 fields available to be used in email templates.

Kind regards.

entry.title: Re: How can S/4HANA Cloud(Public) connect to non-SAP syst...
entry.content:
Hi SAPERPNewbie,

Yes, I believe the Cloud Connector is the correct way to go.

Important to know that the Cloud Connector is installed inside your network and creates a tunnel that can connect to SAP Cloud solutions.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition doesn't run on BTP, it is a separate solution, so I don't expect you would find a subaccount for S/4HANA Cloud. You would need to set up destinations and a comm arrangement ( SAP_COM_0574) in S/4HANA Cloud. (see the help below)

Here is the Help for Integrating OnPrem systems (including Non-SAP) https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/82c9f05663594e5db8f923f6f88ae9bd.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

and a couple good blogs about the Cloud Connector

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/a-complete-guide-to-sap-cloud-connector-concepts-system-requirements-and/ba-p/14283064

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/cloud-connector-explained-in-simple-terms/ba-p/13547036

Could you let us know what type of outbound communication are you interested in? For example event notifications via the Event Mesh (in BTP) or are you looking for Data Replication, etc.?

Thank you
Jerry

entry.title: S/4HANA CDS View Dependency Navigator
entry.content:
If you need S/4HANA data for your analytical project, or you want to understand S/4HANA logic—whatever your S/4HANA information needs might be—you will need CDS views. But how do you find CDS views? You can use the Virtual Data Model and CDS Views page, which provides some information, or simply use my CDS View Dependency Navigator for a more flexible navigation across all CDS views. If this is what you need, then my new blog is for you.

You start your navigation from a selection screen on which you specify a starting CDS view or DDIC table and the navigation direction:

To navigate along the hierarchy and display source code, you click on the desired CDS view/DDIC table in the Name and Source Code columns, respectively. If at any step of your CDS view dependency navigation you take a wrong turn, no problem—just press the F3 Back button to go back. 

As you can see, the Dependency Navigator displays:

  • Dependencies
  • Navigation Path
  • Source Code
  • Important annotations such as Description, Lifecycle Status, Successor, etc.

With the Navigation Path, you will never get lost or lose what you have found. This is how to read the Navigation Path:

  • CDS views up the hierarchy are on your right, and CDS views/DDIC tables down the hierarchy are on your left.
  • The ← and → arrows indicate the navigation direction.

If the end of the CDS view dependency hierarchy is reached, an informational message will be displayed:

If you navigate down the dependency hierarchy and need to see the table structure, it can be done as well:

What else is worth mentioning is that the Navigator’s dependency scope is limited to only the CDS views/DDIC tables used in the selection and only one level up/down, without any associations. This eliminates unnecessary clutter and makes navigation easier. Compare the ADT Dependency Analyzer with the Dependency Navigator for the P_SalesAnalytics CDS view:

I have to acknowledge that the S/4HANA CDS View Dependency Navigator was inspired by the ABAP CDS View Object Flow Program. With the introduction of CDS entities in S/4HANA 2020 (ABAP 7.55), it became obsolete due to missing information about CDS entities. This is because the ABAP CDS View Object Flow Program relied on CDS view DDIC views (the sqlViewName annotation) to navigate along the CDS view hierarchy, which is missing for CDS entities.

I had to rethink the approach to finding dependencies to support both CDS views and CDS entities. The S/4HANA CDS View Dependency Navigator parses CDS view code. All it takes is some knowledge of CDS view syntax and regular expressions (regex). The only complication I encountered when parsing CDS view annotations is the mix of structured and flattened syntax, but this can be addressed by processing all combinations.

Now you know what to do if you need to add more CDS view annotation attributes to the Dependency Navigator. Here is the Dependency Navigator code—enjoy

entry.title: BiWhy Jobs – Make Jobs Work for You, Not Against You
entry.content:
Disclaimer: BiWhy is available again for FREE to everyone on biwhy.net

User load usually builds gradually. If something goes wrong, users report it quickly – either as errors or slow refreshes.

Background job load is different. It may stay unnoticed for a long time while quietly consuming CPU, memory, and database resources.

Typical job problems:

  • too many jobs hitting the system at once
  • long-running data dumps with little real value – the output is too large to open or too slow to use
  • scheduled reports that fail after wasting resources

At the same time, jobs are extremely valuable when managed well. Some companies run reporting workloads 24/7 and save end users tons of time.

BiWhy now provides analytics for Future, Running, and History jobs.

Future

Analytics show when load will hit you – by day, day of week, day of month, or hour – and who is generating it, by user, report, or project.

Running

Analytics provide the usual BiWhy view of currently running jobs.

History

Analytics show failure patterns over time – including jobs that never finish successfully, or jobs above a failure rate you define.

 

In one recent case, I found 611 reports that had never finished successfully in the first four months of 2026.

Together, they accounted for about 7,130 failed instances and 172 days of processing time. The real number is 2–3 times higher, since the system keeps only the last 100 instances.

About 50 of those reports ran daily or even more often. Around 100 had an average runtime above 30 minutes – most in hours, some in days.

And that is only elapsed processing time. In real compute terms, the cost is much higher: those jobs were burning many CPUs in parallel, wasting years of compute and producing exactly ZERO useful outcome.
Some of them were also bringing down BOBJ nodes almost daily.

Practical cleanup tips:

  • 100% of sibling instances failing – likely a report issue
  • Some sibling instances succeed while others fail – likely a schedule-specific issue, such as overly broad prompt values
  • Frequently running reports – more executions, more waste
  • Long-running reports – fewer executions, but higher cost per failure

Tidy up your jobs. Spread them properly. Remove the ones nobody needs. Fix the ones that frequently fail.

Use BiWhy to make jobs work for you, not against you.

Future jobs load by time

Failing jobs table

entry.title: SAP DRC Configuration
entry.content:
Hello Community,

Looking for SAP DRC Configuration and process.

Anyone can brief it like functional SD and FI configuration and BTP configuration.

Means a lot

I have analyzed few things related to this sharing with you all but still configuration steps and BTO configuration steps required.

Bullet Points 

SAP DRC Definition And Usage

SAP DRC High Level Architecture 

Supported Compliance Tasks by Country or Region

KSA Supported Scenarios

SAP DRC Cloud Edition Activation Steps

Steps 

SAP DRC Cloud Edition is hosted on SAP BTP and connect backend ERP (S/4HANA Cloud, S/4HANA On-Prem) to local tax/government authorities .

Pre-requisites

  1. BTP Global Account CPEA or Pay-as-you-go
  2. Subaccount with Entitlement to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition

Activation Steps :

you can run BTP Booster for automatic setup or use below Manual Setup .

1. Entitle the Service in BTP Cockpit

  • Go to your BTP Global Account
  • Navigate to the Subaccount you want to use
  • Open Entitlements Configure Entitlements
  • Add Service Plan: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition
  • Select default or the appropriate service plan .

2. Create Service Instance and Service Key

  • Go to Instances and Subscriptions in your BTP Subaccount

 

Click Create → Service Instance

Choose: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition

Choose the plan (default) , Set a name

  • After creation, Create Service Key

You’ll use the key (credentials, endpoint) to connect your ERP .

3. Setup Trust & Connectivity

  • If ERP is in S/4HANA Public Cloud: no middleware needed
  • For On-Premise or Private Cloud, set up:

 

-SAP Cloud Connector (to connect backend)

-Trust relationship with SAP Identity Authentication (IAS) if needed

-SAP Integration Suite if required by the scenario

4. Configure Communication Arrangement in ERP

In S/4HANA Cloud:

Use SSCUI or CBC to activate relevant scope items (e.g., 5XU, 5XN, etc.)

Create Communication Arrangements to connect ERP to DRC Cloud via APIs

In S/4HANA On-Premise:

Use SOAMANAGER or manually set up RFC destinations / HTTP connections

-Activate required BC Sets and Business Functions .

Looking forward to hearing you step by step configuration and BTP setup.

Thanks,

Ashish

entry.title: How to Retrigger or correct I-9 - Onboarding
entry.content:
ealing with compliance in SAP SuccessFactors is usually straightforward—until a New Hire enters their Social Security Number or personal details incorrectly on their US Form I-9.🤦‍

If you've tried using the standard "Restart Compliance Process" object, you’ve likely hit a wall: it supports almost all compliance forms except the I-9.

So, how do you fix an I-9 error without deleting the entire onboarding process? The secret lies in a specific object: Trigger I-9 Flow.

✨Step-by-Step

Step 1: Initiating the New Flow

  1. Navigate to Manage Data > Create New.

  2. Select the object Trigger I-9 Flow.

  3. User: Enter the employee’s name.

  4. Trigger Type: Select the appropriate type (e.g., I-9 Reverification or Section 1).

  5. Process ID: Enter the ID of the onbaordee.

  6. Save.

A new task will automatically be assigned to the employee or the designated I-9 Responsible user.

 

✨Now that you’ve started a new flow, you’ll have an old, incorrect task lingering in the system. To avoid confusion and data duplication, you must cancel it:

Step 2: Deleting previous Process

  1. Go to Manage Data and search for Compliance Process.

  2. Locate the specific record using the Compliance Process ID or User ID.

  3. Under Take Action, select Make Correction.

  4. Change the Compliance Process Status to Cancelled.

  5. Save.

entry.title: VS Code: Command MDK: Open Mobile App Editor not found when working with SAP MDK project
entry.content:
Description
I am working on an SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) application using Visual Studio Code and the MDK VS Code extension.

When I try to execute the command:

MDK: Open Mobile App Editor
(from the Command Palette or via a documented workflow), VS Code displays the error:

Command 'mdk:openMobileAppEditor' not found

The command does not appear in the Command Palette either.

My Environment
Visual Studio Code:1.118.1
MDK VS Code Extension: 1.32.1
OS: Windows

Question
Is mdk.openMobileAppEditor still a valid command in the current MDK VS Code extension, or has it been deprecated / renamed?
If deprecated, what is the recommended way to open or manage the MDK Mobile App Editor functionality in VS Code?
Are there specific prerequisites or activation conditions required for this command to be registered?
Any guidance or confirmation from someone who has faced this issue would be really helpful.

entry.title: Re: Sales Order creation via EDI using API - New Customer...
entry.content:
Yes, this is expected behavior when CMIR is missing. SAP does not provide a standard way to “fix” TATX lines. The best practice is to stop them at source (middleware/API) or provide a custom reprocessing path for CSRs.

Enhance your middleware or API mapping logic to check if the buyer part number exists in CMIR before creating the order. If missing, route the order to an error queue instead of creating a TATX line. This way, CSR can fix the CMIR and reprocess the inbound message.

The other option is to use BAdIs like SD_SALES_DOCUMENT_CREATE to intercept order creation. If a buyer part number is found without CMIR, raise an error or park the order rather than creating TATX. This prevents “useless” lines from entering the system.

entry.title: Re: Co-Product Cost Estimate Not Matching Apportionment S...
entry.content:
Why the system values differ is because of the following:

1) Apportionment structure
You have defined 60% for header material 31000001 and 40% for co‑product 31000005.

2) System calculation
It gave 184 vs. 136, which is a 57.5% / 42.5% split — not your maintained percentages.

3) Reason
In joint production, the system considers not only the apportionment structure but also factors like lot size, equivalence numbers, and valuation settings. If equivalence numbers or lot sizes are not aligned with your apportionment percentages, the distribution will deviate.

Having said that, the system is behaving as designed, but the deviation suggests either equivalence numbers or lot size are not aligned with your intended percentages. Adjusting those should bring the distribution back to 192/128.

entry.title: Re: VS Code: Command MDK: Open Mobile App Editor not foun...
entry.content:
Hi @aelsiar,

I've seen this happen after recent updates to the MDK VS Code Extension. The mdk:openMobileAppEditor command is sometimes hidden from the Command Palette if the extension hasn't 'seen' an MDK file yet.

Quick fix:

  • Open your Application.app file.

  • Instead of the Palette, right-click the file in the Explorer and look for 'MDK: Open Editor'.

  • Also, check if the MDK Activity Bar icon is visible on the left. Opening the editor through that view is now the preferred 'Clean Core' workflow for SAP Mobile Services.

In my research on Smart Ports, we rely heavily on these editors to build real-time asset tracking screens—if the UI doesn't activate, usually it's because the MDKProject.json is missing from the root directory!"

Are you working on a project migrated from the SAP Web IDE, or was this created directly within VS Code using the MDK template?

entry.title: Re: Split Valuation with Automatic Batch (Category ‘X’) –...
entry.content: Always specify the batch when creating STOs for materials with valuation category ‘X’. If operationally you don’t know the batch at STO creation, consider redesigning the process to use batch determination so the system can assign it automatically. Without batch, the STO cannot resolve valuation type, so this is expected behavior rather than a bug.

entry.title: Re: How to Retrigger or correct I-9 - Onboarding
entry.content:
Thank you, @FrancoTornaghi155! This is a lifesaver for many admins.

From my perspective in Industrial Systems, handling the I-9 retrigger via Manage Data instead of a full 'Process Restart' is the ultimate 'Clean Core' move. It respects the existing onboarding structure while surgically fixing the compliance error.

A quick tip for Step 1: When selecting the Trigger Type, ensure you distinguish between Section 1 (for employee corrections) and Reverification (for expiring documents). Using the wrong trigger type can lead to the wrong task appearing in the employee's dashboard.

Question: Have you found that this method automatically updates the E-Verify status, or do you still find it necessary to manually trigger a new E-Verify case once Section 2 of the new I-9 flow is completed?

entry.title: Re: SAP DRC Configuration
entry.content:
Great summary, @Ashish13! You’ve captured the essential BTP prerequisites perfectly. From an Industrial Engineering perspective, the transition to SAP DRC is a 'mechanical necessity' for global trade. For the KSA (ZATCA) scenario you mentioned, the integration often hinges on the correct handling of Master Data—specifically ensuring that the cryptographic stamp onboarding is performed correctly in the DRC Cloud Edition app before the first transmission.

One technical addition for your configuration steps:

When setting up Step 4 (Communication Arrangements) in S/4HANA Cloud, don't forget to check the specific Company Code Settings for DRC. You must link your local reporting entity to the country-specific reporting category to ensure the data maps correctly from your SD/FI postings to the legal XML.

Quick question: For the KSA implementation, are you planning to use the standard eDocument Cockpit for manual monitoring, or are you looking to automate the submission process entirely via background jobs?

entry.title: Re: Co-Product Cost Estimate Not Matching Apportionment S...
entry.content:
Hi @Krishna2198,

This is a classic 'Equivalence vs. Percentage' conflict in Controlling (CO). While you've maintained the structure as 60/40, the system calculates the split based on the Equivalence Numbers derived during the costing run.

From an Industrial Systems Engineering perspective, this 57.5/42.5 split suggests the system is adjusting for a specific variable—most likely Lot Size or Rounding in the Equivalence Number calculation.

To fix this:

  • Go to the Production Version of material 31000001 and explicitly check the 'Apportionment Structure' button. Ensure the Equivalence Numbers are set to exactly 6 for the header and 4 for the co-product.

  • Check if there are any Direct Costs in your BOM that are marked as specifically belonging to the co-product; these bypass the apportionment structure entirely.

In my research on Maritime Logistics, we treat these splits like 'Payload Distributions'—if the base units don't align, the final balance will always shift!

entry.title: Re: I am trying to run the inventory audit report but non...
entry.content:
Hi David,

This is standard behavior in SAP Business One: Drop Ship warehouses are non-stock locations used only for logistics, so even if a GRPO is posted to them, the system accepts the document but does not update inventory quantities or values and does not create any valuation entries. That’s why the received units don’t appear in the Item Master Data and why the warehouse is not available in inventory reports like the Inventory Audit Report.

entry.title: Re: The Quiet Revolution in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Logistics: Clean Core, Transparency, and AI
entry.content:
Hi @Melih,

Thank you for the thoughtful perspective. You raise a fair point about legacy systems—they have been the robust backbone of supply chains for decades, and my intention isn't to dismiss their immense capabilities.

My reference to "messy backend modifications" is specifically regarding the long-term technical debt that historically accumulated in traditional on-premise environments. Often, what started as "light customization" compounded over years of upgrades, creating complex maintenance challenges. In the context of S/4HANA Public Cloud, the strict governance around Key User Extensibility and specific BAdIs acts as a safeguard. It enforces a discipline that ensures our enhancements remain upgrade-safe and resilient by design, shifting us away from the highly flexible but sometimes perilous enhancement frameworks of the past.

I'd be interested to hear how your teams are navigating the transition to this more strictly governed extensibility model.

entry.content:

This is actually a very real problem, especially after go-live when clients operate across multiple tenants (DEV, QA, PRD). From my experience, once things scale, it becomes difficult to keep visibility across tenants, especially when trying to understand what’s different or out of sync.

The orchestration layer you described makes a lot of sense from a delivery perspective. One gap I’ve consistently seen, though, is around comparison and drift visibility across tenants. Today, there isn’t an easy or native way to compare artifacts or packages between systems without a lot of manual effort.

I’m currently working on a tool that focuses specifically on that side cross-tenant comparison at the package level to highlight differences and help teams quickly identify drift between environments.

Curious how others are currently handling this, or if they’re just relying on manual checks.

entry.title: Re: Billing Document created but no eDocument appears in...
entry.content:
Hello!

 

An eDocument is supposed to be created automatically, but only if specific country and master‑data conditions are met.

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, an eDocument instance is created automatically at the moment the billing document is posted, only when the billing document qualifies for a country‑specific e‑invoicing scenario delivered by SAP.

If those conditions are not met, the billing document is still posted successfully, but no eDocument is created at all. In that case, nothing appears in:

  • Manage Electronic Documents (F4306)
  • eDocument Cockpit (EDOC_COCKPIT)

This is standard SAP behavior and explains why there are no records or error messages for the invoice.

 

The Central Business Configuration (CBC) activities you completed are necessary, but they are only technical enablers. They do not by themselves trigger eDocument creation. For an eDocument to be created, SAP also evaluates:

  • The company code country
  • Whether that country has SAP‑delivered e‑invoicing localization
  • Whether company and customer master data meet the country‑specific requirements

If any of these conditions are not satisfied, the document is considered out of scope for eDocument processing and is skipped silently.

 

In the vast majority of similar cases, the root cause is missing or incorrect country‑specific identification data, specifically:

Company Code:

  • Must belong to a country supported by SAP e‑invoicing localization
  • Must have the required Party Identification Type (for example, VAT) assigned

Customer (Business Partner)

  • Must have a valid country‑specific identification (for example, VAT number)
  • The identification type must match what SAP expects for that country

If either the company or the customer does not have the correct party ID maintained, SAP will not create an eDocument, even though:

  • The billing document is posted successfully
  • CBC configuration is complete
  • eDocument types are assigned in CBC

 

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud:

  • If an eDocument is created, errors are visible in Error Analysis
  • If an eDocument is not eligible, no record is created at all

That is why no errors are shown, no logs are visible - the document is absent from the eDocument app.

To fix the issue is recommended to validate the followings:

  1. Company code
    • Country is supported for SAP e‑invoicing
    • Correct party identification type is assigned
  1. Customer master data
  • Valid country‑specific VAT / registration number maintained
  • Correct identification type used
  1. Billing document
  • Billing type is mapped to the correct country‑specific eDocument type

 

Additional documentation:

  • At the following link you will find a SAP course about the Creating Electronic Document and Legal Report:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/applying-localization-as-a-self-service-for-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/creating-electronic-document-and-legal-report

  • At the following link you will find a similar question addressed on SAP Community:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/no-edocument-created-in-ecockpit-sap-public-cloud/qaq-p/14278671
